transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Okay team — quick debrief from the client scope call that just ended. Vinod sir, do you want to start?

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: I'll give the summary. They pushed back initially — as expected. But Rakesh's effort document was very detailed and well structured. I showed it to them and they couldn't dispute the numbers. Credit to Rakesh for that.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Good to hear. What's the final agreement?

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Sprint 5 is being split into two phases. Phase A — inventory sync and returns flow, 3 weeks. Phase B — admin dashboard, 3 additional weeks. That's the phasing they agreed to.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Okay that's more reasonable.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: But there's one more thing — and this is important. The technical risk document Rakesh and Varsha prepared. About the async retry queue being missing and the 2 intermittent prod failures. Client read it carefully. Their response was — and I'm quoting — 'You're currently running production with a known failure mode. That async retry queue is not Sprint 5 item 1. It's item 0. Before anything else.'

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: Item 0 — meaning before inventory sync, before returns flow, before everything?

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Yes. They want it done first. They were not happy that it was descoped in Sprint 4. Honestly — fair point.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: I agree it's fair. Varsha — how long for the async queue done properly? With unit tests, not just working code.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: 4 days. But I need the actual queue infrastructure — Redis or RabbitMQ. I can write the code against a mock but testing needs real infra.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Redis setup is half a day for me. But — same story as last time — I need central infra to approve it. New infrastructure component requires their sign-off.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Vinod sir — can you please send an escalation email to the infra team today? Last time when Vivek raised the SSL ticket himself it took 2 days. When the mail comes from you it gets done faster.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Yes. Send me the details Vivek, I'll send the mail within the hour.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Sending now.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I also want to raise — can we please get a proper prod-parity staging environment? The whole SSL cert situation last week, the config differences — it happened because staging and prod are maintained separately by different teams. I've been asking for this for 3 sprints.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Same request from my side. Three sprints I've been saying this.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: I'll include it in the same mail to infra. Ajay give me a 3-line requirement — exactly what you need.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I'll message you in 10 minutes.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: One last thing — client also confirmed formal UAT starts next Monday. Their full payments team. They want to test everything we have — payment flow, edge cases, the works.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: Monday is 5 days away. And async queue isn't even started.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: UAT is for the payment flow we already delivered, not async queue. Async queue will make the existing flow more stable, which is good for UAT. But UAT scope is Sprint 4 deliverables.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: Okay okay. Got it.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: Alright. Clear plan — Vinod sir sends infra mail today. Vivek sets up Redis the moment approval comes. Varsha starts async queue code today with mock. Ajay begins UAT prep in parallel. Monday is the target.
